steps.title: Identify what the question needs
steps.content: Alegado and colleagues concluded that neither V. fischeri nor B. fragilis likely played a significant role in the development of multicellular organisms. Their reasoning links bacteria to multicellularity through colony formation: if S. rosetta does not form colonies in response to a bacterium, that bacterium was not a trigger for the multicellular transition. A supporting finding must show that S. rosetta did not form colonies with either bacterium.

steps.title: Eliminate the choices
steps.content:
• "S. rosetta tended to form colonies when exposed to V. fischeri and when exposed to B. fragilis.": Colony formation with both bacteria would suggest both played a role in multicellularity — this directly contradicts the conclusion. ✗
  • "S. rosetta tended to form colonies when exposed to V. fischeri, but not when exposed to B. fragilis.": Colony formation with V. fischeri would implicate that bacterium in multicellularity, contradicting the conclusion that neither played a role. ✗
  • "S. rosetta tended to form colonies when exposed to B. fragilis, but not when exposed to V. fischeri.": Colony formation with B. fragilis would implicate that bacterium, contradicting the conclusion. ✗
  • "S. rosetta did not tend to form colonies when exposed to either V. fischeri or B. fragilis.": No colony formation with either bacterium means neither triggered the multicellular response, which directly supports the conclusion that neither played a significant role in the development of multicellularity. ✓
That matches the correct answer.
